title: Repairing a popped plasterboard nail
subtitle: Re-secure a plasterboard sheet at a popped fixing and reskim the patch
excerpt: How to deal with a plasterboard nail or screw head that has worked through the surface, leaving a small bump and a hairline crack. Covers driving a new fixing alongside the popped one, removing the original head, filling and feathering, and re-decorating.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plasterboard fixing that has worked back through the surface as the timber stud behind it shrinks or moves seasonally. The head pushes the paint or skim coat out as a small bump, often with a hairline crack around it.
A short horizontal timber fixed between two studs to provide a fixing point and stop the wall flexing. Plasterboard fixings hit a noggin where the sheet edge falls between studs.

content.text: is the most common plasterboard defect in a house older than five years. Seasonal movement in the stud or

behind the sheet pushes the fixing forward against the back of the board. The head appears as a small bump or a fine ring crack in the paint. The fix is short: secure the sheet with a new fixing alongside the original, drive the original head below the surface so it cannot push out again, then fill and decorate.
Step 1, drive a new screw 50 mm above or below the pop

Set a drywall screw 50 mm above or below the popped head, into the same stud or noggin. Use a 38 mm drywall screw for 12.5 mm plasterboard, 50 mm for double-board or for fixing through a skim coat. Drive the screw until the head sits just below the paper face without breaking the paper. A drywall-screw setter bit stops the screw at the right depth; a standard Phillips bit works with a careful hand on the trigger.
Step 2, drive the original head below the surface

If the popped fixing is a nail, hold a small nail punch on the head and tap it with the hammer until the head sits 2 mm below the paper face. If it is a screw, drive it back in with the drill, sinking the head 2 mm below the surface. Do not try to pull the original fixing out. Removing it tears the paper face and turns a small repair into a large patch.
Step 3, fill both heads and the ring crack

Press one-coat lightweight filler into both screw heads with the 50 mm filling knife. Push filler into the hairline crack around the original pop, pressing it through the surface paint into the gap. Draw a clean stroke across the wall to leave the filler proud by about a millimetre. Allow to dry; the wait depends on filler grade and air temperature. A typical one-coat filler stands at 1 to 2 hours; check the bag.
Step 4, feather the edge and sand flush

Apply a second thin pass with the 100 mm filling knife, working the filler outward 100 mm beyond each repair. The wide knife feathers the edge into the surrounding wall so the patch sits flat under the new paint. Sand with 180 grit on a sanding block, then 240 grit, using even pressure. The patch should disappear under a flat hand stroke.

Spot-prime each repair with the wall paint diluted ten per cent with water, allow to dry, then re-coat the whole wall in one even roll. A single spot of fresh paint over a filler patch reads as a flash mark under raked light; a full wall coat blends the patch invisibly. On a textured or stippled wall, dab the wet final coat with a sponge to mimic the texture before it tacks.

On a row of pops down a stud line, treat them as one job: drive replacement screws above and below the whole row, sink every original head, then run filler down the line as a single strip and feather both edges. Repaint the full wall in one session.

attrs.symptom: The pop returns within a few months
attrs.cause: The replacement screw missed the stud and is holding in plasterboard alone, or the original timber is moving more than expected (a damp leak in the cavity behind, a stud near a heat source).
attrs.fix: Confirm the stud position with a detector. If the cavity is dry and the stud is solid, accept a slightly longer screw (50 to 65 mm) for more bite. If the timber keeps moving, investigate damp ingress or heat source as a root cause.

attrs.symptom: The paper face tore around the original head when filling
attrs.cause: The original fixing was driven through the paper face and the filler caught the loose edge.
attrs.fix: Trim the loose paper flat with a stanley knife, fill the resulting shallow patch with two thin coats of filler, sand flush. If the tear is larger than a 50 pence piece, treat as a plasterboard hole patch.

attrs.symptom: The filler patch is visible as a flash mark under raked light after painting
attrs.cause: The filler was not sanded flush, or the wall was spot-primed with full-strength wall paint rather than a thinned mist coat.
attrs.fix: Re-sand the patch with 240 grit, dust off, brush on a thinned (ten per cent water) coat of wall paint as a mist, then re-coat the whole wall.
